text: This presentation is for the single port robotic transanal minimally invasive surgery or SB-TAMIS technique. We have no disclosures. The first case will demonstrate the basic principles of this technique. And the second case will demonstrate the capabilities of this technique.

The first case is a fifty nine year old male with unresectable polyp on screening colonoscopy. This was a three centimeter tubular villus adenoma, sixteen centimeters from the anal verge. who otherwise had normal imaging.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy with the SP robot docked between the legs. A gel port is inserted to protect the anal canal, and a single port trocar is docked alongside an assistant port connected to continuous CO₂ insufflation.

The robotic camera provides a view of the lesion, while an assist port is used to introduce tools like suction. Robotic arms are docked under direct visualization, with their movements tracked by an animation displayed at the bottom of the screen.

The surgeon marks the boundaries of resection on the rectum using monopolar electrocautery. A full thickness dissection is performed until the perirectal fat is visible. Instruments are introduced through the assist port to provide counter traction, stabilizing the floppy rectum. The flap of rectum is dissected out along the marked boundaries, with frequent checks to ensure the proximal margin is reached without excessive resection.

The assistant grasps the resected specimen to maintain its orientation and removes it through the robotic port. The surgeon begins closure of the defect using two barbed absorbable sutures, starting at both ends and suturing transversely to prevent narrowing of the rectal lumen.

You can see from the animation on the bottom of the screen that the robotic arms are fully deployed and functioning very analogous to the traditional robotic TAMIS tools in this capacity. If the arms experience any external tension, the color of the arms change from white to yellow and eventually to red. We will see the relevance of this in the second case to be presented.

The assistant cuts and removes the sutures, followed by a completion proctoscopy that reveals the defect has been sutured closed. A comparison image shows the defect before the operation, marking the completion of the case.

The video shows a fifty-year-old female patient with a large, five-centimeter mid-rectal sessile mass, identified as a tubular villus adenoma. The mass is located closer to the dentate line, with the borders of the trans-anal gel port visible during visualization.

The robotic arms are shown with a red color animation on the screen, indicating restricted deployment due to the gel port canal. The surgeon adjusts the camera and makes small movements to redirect the operative area, allowing the procedure to continue despite the limited space.

The surgical platform rotates to provide a top-down view of the anterior rectal lesion, with water pooling above. The camera's serpiginous motion enhances proximal visualization of the rectal tumor, overcoming obstruction issues typical with conventional robotic systems. The surgeon marks the margins and begins full-thickness dissection of the rectum.

The surgeon performs distal dissection near the anal verge, a challenging area due to limited space for robotic arm deployment. Despite this, precise operations are conducted by adjusting the camera view to enhance visibility and maneuverability.

The robotic choke arm moves to redirect the surgical area, allowing the robotic arms to fully deploy and operate normally in the proximal rectum. The animation at the bottom of the screen shows the arms deployed, no longer in red color. The surgeon checks the proximal margin to ensure the sample size is appropriate, preventing the defect from enlarging.

The surgeon removes the fully dissected mid-rectal mass through the assist port, ensuring proper orientation for potential frozen section analysis. Despite the large defect, the surgeon uses two barbed absorbable sutures to close the defect transversely.

It cannot be stressed enough the importance of continuous CO₂ insufflation instead of the traditional pulsatile CO₂ insufflation during this case to prevent the bellowing of the colon at this very critical time.
The video shows a completion view of the surgical defect, indicating the end of the procedure.
